All right, let's rock and roll. So, lead gen videos. There are different types of videos I'm gonna go over so that you understand the strategy and the taxes behind making videos that are going to convert for you. All right, and so there's two primary objectives you have with a video. You're gonna have pure opt-in videos, which the two types that have worked best, historically are group workout videos and pitch videos. I'm gonna show you examples of each of those after looking at literally millions of dollars of spending, which one worked the best, and I'll show you just how you can deconstruct it. What's really cool about both of these is that there's a formula that is really repeatable that you can use over and over again for each of them, which is exciting for me after looking at all the data. All right, the next is, if you want like higher quality applications. So these are always typically gonna be more costly, and there's two styles here that I recommend. Both of them tend to convert well. It just gives you a little bit different look and feel. The next is long form adjugate videos. I wanna be clear, these are the hardest ones to make because they just take the most time, they take the most scripting, it has to be really useful. All right, so for opt-ins, because this is where the majority of people literally make their money, I wanna cover this first. And so for this, I wanna cover the six questions, what, why, when, where, who, and how, all right? So what, so this is what I wanna show you right now, are these are the top video ads moving in synchrony, in unison, the same thing at the same time draws people's attention, and it just freaking works, right? You can see it right here. These are all the ads that have converted best that fall into the first category. And I just wanna be clear that 75, 80% of the best ads, total have been these style ads. And the nice thing is that these are some of the easiest ones to make and capture, all right? Here's some more. So I like showing these, because there's different look and feels that I want you to see the same where people are doing it. So here's the formula for it, all right? It's synchrony, social proof, the reason it's social proof is because you're showing that you have a lot of people, so the more people doing it, the better. It's also from a human mentality, we want to do what everyone else is doing, and we want to fit in, we want to be in the in group, and so subconsciously, people are drawn to those types of things and want to be included in it, all right? You also see that there's energy, there's movement. This is really important. The things that do best are the ones where people are moving together, not just like sitting in a circle together or doing wall sits together, but actually squatting, doing pushups, doing bicycles, with their bicycle setups or whatever. Things that are movement, and the more in synchrony they can be, the more effective they will be as an advertisement, right? Also shows community because when people do things together, it means they are in, well, synchrony, but there's a relationship, and so it truly shows a group better than almost anything on a subconscious animal level, and that is why this stuff works, and as much as you're like, why is Alex talking about animal and evolution and all that kind of stuff, it's because you're amygdala, which is the tiny little reptile brain in your brain, is the one that makes all of your decisions for you, and then you rationalize them later, and so these are the things that that little brain is looking for to say, hey, that looks good, we should do that, all right? Now after the ABCD of why these work, the last thing is that you always want to have clear call to actions, and that goes for any advertisement, but it's just worth mentioning again. All right, so what are the who? Having a cheap old iPhone or iPad on the class floor requiring your trainers to record the finishers or any group exercises, and trying to get people to do it on the same cadence by doing countdowns and whatnot works very, very well. People also like it a lot too. It keeps everyone together, so you kind of build community while you're doing it, and you can record advertisements. All right, now, if you have them do this at every one of their sessions, you should be getting six to eight new videos per day. This is called a process. If we do this on a continual basis, you don't need to spend all this time trying to collect and make ads for yourself if this is just part of the process at your facility. If you get six to eight new ads a day, you're gonna have 40 by the end of the week, and you will be able to find five or six really good ones where there was a lot more people, there was good energy, and people are really doing things in unison. All right, one thing that I would do here is I would try and take these from different angles. So every day I would have the different trainers take it from a specific angle, so that can be from in the middle doing a loop, it can be from the corner, so you can see the whole class, it can be from an elevated surface. You wanna take from one side of the gym, from another side of the gym. You wanna walk into the gym while people are doing it. All of those are different variations of the same concept, but will make the ads look somewhat different for you. It can be you and if it's not you, it needs to be this, which is a good looking trainer. Women tend to do better than men. And what I wanna say here is like, I'm purely talking advertising. I do not favor men over women, women over men. It is irrelevant to me. I'm just looking at data. There is a reason that advertising agencies hire models. There's a reason that big brands hire models for their commercials because good looking people tend to convert better than not good looking people. And especially when what you're selling is being good looking, which is in some ways what fitness is, right? So some other things you might wanna notice is that bright color clothing tends to do best. It's just bright, it's movement, it's color. It also tends to evoke happier thoughts. All right, now, where are you gonna be doing this? Here are different versions of making this happen. In the gym when it's empty, works. I did an entire series of ads one night for two hours, and I held all the classes, and they had to be silent with no music. It was like the weirdest thing, it was horrible, don't make that mistake.
